system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: Essential Chords, Synonyms

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 7 — Essential Chords, Synonyms

_Pages 13-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Polychord viewpoint

> The idea of polychords is given here just to present you with another viewpoint or way of seeing
>
> chord construction.

**Significance (p. 12).** Frames polychord theory as an alternative analytical lens.

## Polychord construction

> There are quite a few chords that consist of 2 triads or parts of 2 triads. Usually the notes of |
>
> triad are superimposed on top of another triad although the notes of both triads maybe jumpled up.

**Significance (p. 12).** Defines polychords as superimposed triads.

## Bottom triad may omit 5th

> In many cases the bottom triad may leave out the Sth.

**Significance (p. 12).** Practical voicing rule for polychord realization.

## Polychord use in ensemble

> This polytonal type of logic is useful if you are playing with another guitar player, bass player,
> piano player or any other musician involved with creating the harmonic foundation of a piece of
> music.

**Significance (p. 12).** Specifies ensemble context where polychord thinking applies.

## Bass + upper triad voicing rule

> For an All sound, the bass player can play an A bass note and you could play G triads. (When
> you are superimposing triads on a bass note, it is usually best to avoid using low sounding
> voicings; that is, stick to medium to very high notes and let the bass player’s note carry the
> bottom.)

**Significance (p. 12).** Worked example with voicing rule: keep upper triads high when bassist plays root.

## Essential chords sufficient

> These have been found
> to be more than enough chords to meet the needs of just about any musical situation; so these chords
> should be studied first and memorized gradually in terms of complexity

**Significance (p. 13).** Pedagogical anchor: the essential set covers most musical needs.

## Order of complexity for learning

> Learn the major
> and minor chords first, then the major 6th and minor 6th, then the major 7th, minor 7th, and dominant
> 7th, etc. all the way up to the 13 + 11, 6/9 + 11, and such.

**Significance (p. 13).** Explicit progression order from simple to complex chords.

## Chord knowledge requires application

> it would be senseless to know many chords and not
> know where to use them.

**Significance (p. 13).** Method principle prioritizing application over collection.

## Look for physically related chords

> One more thing — try to look for chords that are closely related physically.

**Significance (p. 13).** Memorization heuristic based on physical proximity of voicings.

## Physical relation aids memorization

> If you can relate to chords in this manner, it

**Significance (p. 13).** Asserts that physical-relation grouping accelerates memorization.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
